title: Christian Science Monitor: Surge in Federal Tort Claims for ICE Excessive Force
excerpt: A review of more than a dozen legal claims filed between June and December 2025 found people alleged immigration agents assaulted or detained them without cause, with injuries including bruises, lacerations, head injuries, and concussions. The filings raised broad questions about federal accountability.
content:
A February 2026 investigation by the Christian Science Monitor reviewed more than a dozen Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) filings dated June through December 2025 in which individuals alleged that ICE and Border Patrol personnel assaulted or detained them without cause.

Claimants reported bruises, lacerations, and head injuries. Almost a dozen people alleged they were tackled or thrown to the ground. Multiple claimants said federal personnel pushed their knees into detainees' faces, necks, or backs; one man said agents pepper-sprayed him before repeatedly punching him in the face and head. In one December 2025 case, Hilda Ramirez Sanan — a permanent resident — alleged ICE officers in unmarked cars failed to identify themselves before forcing her family from a vehicle, then slammed her to the ground, twisting her arms and kicking her leg, resulting in bruising and a concussion.

  • Federal government and its employees are generally shielded by sovereign immunity from being sued for official acts, creating steep barriers to civil accountability
  • Civil rights lawyers noted that the surge in interior enforcement operations had made civilian encounters with federal agents far more common
  • Experts cited the pattern as highlighting a fundamental lack of federal law enforcement accountability
mediaPlaceholder: Copies of FTCA filings documenting injuries suffered during ICE enforcement encounters, June–December 2025
